reasoning: Cayenne is located in French Guiana (4°N), which has an equatorial climate with distinct wet and dry seasons. October is near the end of the dry season and is typically the hottest month of the year, with a mean daily average temperature of approximately 27.5 °C. November begins a transition to slightly cooler temperatures (mean roughly 27.1 °C), and January is solidly in the rainy season, featuring significantly cooler daily averages (approximately 26.1 °C). Because we are comparing the temperatures of future dates to a baseline date in the hottest month (October 26), the expected temperature difference is negative and decreases further as we progress into January. Daily temperature variance in this regions is relatively small (estimated SD of ~0.7 °C for a daily average difference). Consequently, the probability that the daily average temperature on our target dates will simply exceed the October baseline is strictly less than 50% for November 2 and drops substantially lower for November 25 and January 24. A generic heuristic suggests uncertainty should increase over time returning strictly towards 0.5, but physical seasonal forcing fundamentally shifts the expectation further from the baseline, meaning probabilities for distant future dates in the cooler rainy season are robustly far below 0.5.
